title: Text Recognition: CRNN & CTC Loss
concept: CRNNs combine CNNs for visual features and RNNs for sequence modeling, using Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) loss to align outputs without character-level cropping.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main difference between EAST and CRAFT?

options.text: EAST outputs word-level boxes; CRAFT outputs character-level affinity maps.
EAST is only for handwriting; CRAFT is for printed text.
EAST uses recurrent layers; CRAFT does not.

exercises.explain: Correct! EAST detects words as bounding boxes, while CRAFT maps individual characters and connects them.

cards.title: Reading without slicing
cards.body: Once you have cropped a word image (like 'apple'), how does the network read it? You might think we need to cut the image into five neat boxes (one for each letter) and run a classifier on each. But letters in cursive overlap, and space spacing varies. We need a model that reads the word as a continuous sequence.

cards.title: CRNN & CTC Loss
cards.body: A CRNN (Convolutional Recurrent Neural Network) combines a CNN (to extract visual feature sequences from a cropped text strip) and an RNN/LSTM (to model the order of letters). It uses CTC Loss (Connectionist Temporal Classification) to align the network's predictions with the true label, handling variable spacing and repeat characters.

cards.title: Scanning barcode lines
cards.body: Think of a CRNN like a supermarket barcode scanner. The scanner sweeps a laser across the barcode horizontally. It doesn't cut the barcode into separate lines; it reads the continuous sequence of dark and light stripes as it scans, outputting the barcode digits.

cards.title: Mobile credit card scanner
cards.body: When you scan your credit card with an app, a CNN extracts feature slices along the card numbers, an LSTM decodes the digit sequence, and CTC loss translates the raw predictions (like `5-5---2-2` to `52`) into your card number.

cards.title: No manual segmentation needed
cards.body: A common myth is that OCR models must crop every single letter to classify it. Thanks to CTC loss, modern models train on whole-word images directly, automatically aligning letters without any human-labeled letter boundaries.

cards.title: The CRNN Pipeline
cards.body: How an image of a word is converted to text:

left.text:
1. Input image: 'cat'
2. CNN extracts vertical slices
3. LSTM predicts slice characters

right.text:
4. Raw output: 'c-a--t'
5. Collapses repeats and blanks
6. Final string: 'cat'

exercises.prompt: What does Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) loss do in text recognition?

options.text: It aligns raw sequence outputs to the true labels, handling variable letter spacing without character crops
It calculates the bounding box coordinates of the entire page
It calculates the Gram matrix to copy artistic styles
It converts color images to grayscale

exercises.explain: CTC loss enables training sequence models on full-word crops by mapping raw predictions (with blanks and repeats) to target labels.

exercises.prompt: True or False: To train a CRNN model, human annotators must label the exact pixel boundaries of every single letter in the training set.
exercises.explain: False. CTC loss aligns the output sequence automatically, meaning only the full word text label (e.g. 'cat') is required.

exercises.sentence: CRNN architectures combine a CNN for visual features and an ___ for sequence decoding.
exercises.explain: The recurrent neural network (RNN, typically LSTM) models the sequential transitions of letters.

exercises.prompt: Which of these describes the final step of CTC sequence decoding?

options.text: Collapsing repeating characters and removing blank tokens (e.g., 'a--p-p-l-e' -> 'apple')
Applying an affine transformation to rotate the page

exercises.explain: CTC decoding merges adjacent duplicate predictions and removes dummy blank tokens to build the final text string.

exercises.prompt: You are building a license plate scanner. The distance between letters changes depending on the vehicle speed, creating stretched or compressed letters. How should you design the text recognition model?

options.text: Use a CRNN trained with CTC loss, which can handle sequence alignment and variable character spacing naturally.
Crop every letter manually into 10x10 pixel squares and classify them in isolation.
Use simple template matching, hoping characters are always identical.
Disable the recurrent layers and use linear regression on pixel values.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The combination of recurrent sequence modeling and CTC loss allows CRNNs to handle variable spacing and speed-induced distortion easily. Outstanding! Next: modern OCR using attention and transformers.
